title: John 16
subtitle: The Work of the Holy Spirit

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — The Last Supper

sections.header: Verses 1–15 — The Spirit of Truth Will Guide You into All Truth

heb.paragraph: The Spirit's threefold work (v.8) uses elenchō — a legal term meaning to expose the truth of a matter, to convict through cross-examination. The Spirit does not merely inform or teach; he cross-examines the world's assumptions about sin, righteousness, and judgment, exposing each as fundamentally wrong. The world thinks sin is anything but unbelief in Jesus; the Spirit proves it is precisely that. The world thinks righteousness belongs to those who crucified Jesus; the Spirit proves it belongs to the one who rose and went to the Father. The world thinks Jesus stands condemned; the Spirit proves the prince of this world is condemned.

The Spirit "will guide you into all the truth" (v.13) — hodēgeō (to lead the way, to guide on a path). The image is of a guide leading through unfamiliar territory. The Spirit does not hand the disciples a map; he walks with them into truth. The promise is progressive and relational: the Spirit guides into all truth over time, not all at once.

refs.note: Pentecost — the arrival of the Advocate Jesus promised, fulfilling 16:7.
"When the people heard this, they were cut to the heart" — the Spirit's conviction of sin through Peter's Pentecost sermon, fulfilling 16:8-9.
"For those who are led by the Spirit of God are the children of God" — Paul's application of the Spirit's guiding work (16:13).

notes.note: "It is for your good that I am going away" — sumpherei (it benefits, it is advantageous). The same word Caiaphas used cynically (11:50 — "it is good for you that one man die"). Here Jesus uses it pastorally: the departure that looks like abandonment is actually the greatest advantage the disciples could receive. The Spirit who comes is not a consolation prize for the absent Christ; he is the mode in which the glorified Christ becomes universally and internally present.

The threefold conviction is courtroom language: the Spirit as the prosecuting attorney who exposes the world's case against Jesus as false on three counts. (1) Sin: the real sin is unbelief — rejecting the light (3:19). (2) Righteousness: the resurrection vindicates Jesus's claim; the Father's acceptance proves his righteousness. (3) Judgment: the cross is not Jesus's condemnation but the world's — "the prince of this world now stands condemned" (12:31).

"He will guide you into all the truth" — the foundation of the doctrine of the canon. The Spirit's guiding into all truth is the promise that underlies the apostolic witness: what the NT contains is Spirit-guided testimony to Jesus, received and recorded by those to whom this promise was made. The "all truth" is not all truth about everything but all truth about Jesus and his significance (v.14: "he will receive from me what he will make known to you").

"He will glorify me... all that belongs to the Father is mine" — the Spirit's work is consistently self-effacing in one direction (toward Jesus) and comprehensively inclusive in another (drawing from all that the Father has). The trinitarian logic: the Father gives all to the Son; the Son gives all to the Spirit; the Spirit makes it known to the disciples. The entire wealth of the Godhead flows through the Spirit to the believer.

Calvin develops the three convictions in order. On sin: the world thinks sin is measured by external acts; the Spirit reveals that the root of sin is the refusal to acknowledge Jesus as the Son of God — all other sins flow from this. On righteousness: the world crucified Jesus as a criminal; the Spirit vindicates him through the resurrection as the uniquely righteous one. On judgment: the prince of this world's condemnation at the cross is the proof that the world's system of judgment has itself been judged.

Calvin uses this verse, along with 14:26, as the primary Johannine basis for the Spirit's role in Scripture: the apostles' recall of Jesus's teaching (14:26) and the Spirit's guidance into all truth (16:13) together guarantee that the apostolic witness — the NT — is Spirit-guided, reliable, and authoritative. This is not mechanical dictation but intelligent, personal guidance.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: "Prove the world wrong" — the NIV's translation of elenchō as "prove wrong" rather than the traditional "convict" is defensible: the Spirit's work is not merely emotional conviction but the exposure of false verdicts. The world held three wrong positions about Jesus; the Spirit's work corrects all three by the facts of the cross and resurrection.

"He will tell you what is yet to come" — ta erchomena ("the things coming"). This phrase has generated debate: does the Spirit's work of revealing "what is yet to come" refer to the apostolic predictive prophecy (Revelation, eschatological teaching) or simply to the unfolding of Jesus's significance as history proceeds? Most scholars take the former: the Spirit enables the apostles to give reliable teaching about the future, including the end times.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Augustine: "It is for your good that I go away. How so? Because while I was with you bodily, I could only be in one place at a time. When I go, I will send the Spirit, who will be with all of you at once — in Jerusalem, in Rome, in Alexandria, in every corner of the world simultaneously. The Spirit does not have a body to limit him."

Chrysostom: "He will guide you into all the truth. Not some of the truth, not part of it, but all of it. Everything necessary for your salvation, everything necessary for your mission — he will lead you into it. Not all at once, but progressively, as you are able to bear it."

hist.context:
The Three Convictions
The Spirit's work of conviction in vv.8-11 is the most theologically precise description of his public ministry in John. Three areas of the world's wrong thinking are exposed: (1) Sin — the world thinks sin is moral failure; the Spirit proves sin is fundamentally the refusal to believe in Jesus (the root of all sin). (2) Righteousness — the world thinks righteousness belongs to the religious establishment that condemned Jesus; the Spirit proves it belongs to Jesus, proven by the resurrection and ascension. (3) Judgment — the world thinks Jesus is condemned; the Spirit proves the prince of this world is condemned, not Jesus.

"It Is For Your Good That I Am Going Away"
The startling claim of v.7 — that the disciples are better off with the Spirit than with the physical presence of Jesus — is the theological key to Christian life after the ascension. The Spirit's "inside" presence (in you, 14:17) is qualitatively different from and in some sense superior to Jesus's "alongside" presence (with you). The Spirit brings the risen, glorified Christ into permanent, internal relationship with every believer simultaneously — something the physical Jesus, limited by his body and location, could not do.

sections.header: Verses 16–33 — Grief Will Turn to Joy; I Have Overcome the World

heb.paragraph: The perfect tense (nenikikā — I have conquered, with present continuing effect) is one of the most theologically weighted tenses in John. Jesus speaks from the threshold of the passion as if the victory is already complete — because in the divine perspective it is. The present grief of the disciples and the coming cross are both already encompassed by a victory that has been won. The word nikē (victory) appears only here in John's Gospel but will dominate 1 John (2:13-14; 4:4; 5:4-5) and Revelation.

The joy theme (chairō, chara) runs through vv.20-24 — seven uses in five verses. The grief/joy contrast is set up by the childbirth image (v.21): the pain of labour gives way to the joy of birth. The disciples' grief at the cross will give way to the joy of the resurrection. The joy will be permanent ("no one will take away your joy," v.22) — unlike the world's joy, which is circumstantial.

refs.note: "As a pregnant woman about to give birth writhes and cries out in her pain, so were we in your presence, Lord" — the OT birth-pain/new-birth imagery.
"I consider that our present sufferings are not worth comparing with the glory that will be revealed in us" — Paul's application of the grief-into-joy principle.
"For everyone born of God overcomes the world. This is the victory that has overcome the world, even our faith" — John's application of the overcoming theme.
"To the one who is victorious, I will give the right to sit with me on my throne, just as I was victorious" — Revelation's application of Christ's overcoming to the believers who share it.

notes.note: "You will weep and mourn while the world rejoices. You will grieve, but your grief will turn to joy" — the reversal of apparent fortunes is the signature of John's Gospel. What looks like victory (the world's joy at the crucifixion) is defeat; what looks like defeat (the disciples' grief) is the birth-pain of the world's greatest joy. The cross looks like the world winning; Easter reveals it as God winning.

"The Father himself loves you because you have loved me and have believed that I came from God" — direct access to the Father's love is now available to believers. Jesus does not say "I will intercede for you with a reluctant Father"; he says "the Father himself loves you." The love of the Father for believers is direct and personal, not mediated only through the Son's intercession. Prayer "in Jesus's name" is heard by a Father who already loves the one praying.

"In this world you will have trouble. But take heart! I have overcome the world" — the two sides of the Christian reality. The trouble (thlipsis — pressure, tribulation) is not removed; the overcoming is the ground for endurance within it. This verse contains both the honest acknowledgment that suffering is certain and the theological foundation for persevering through it. Jesus does not promise the absence of trouble; he promises the presence of the overcomer.

Calvin uses this verse as the foundation of his theology of persecution: the Christian does not escape trouble but enters it with the assurance that it has been overcome. The world's power over the believer is broken not by removing the trouble but by changing the believer's relationship to it — they are "in me," in the one who has overcome. Calvin notes that this is not passive resignation but active courage: "take heart" is a command, not merely a comfort.

The childbirth image is for Calvin one of the most important pastoral texts in the Gospel: it establishes the principle that suffering is always purposive and transitional, never ultimate. The pain has a purpose (birth); the purpose transforms the pain retrospectively. The Christian's present suffering always has an Easter-shaped outcome beyond it.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: "In that day you will no longer ask me anything" — the "asking" here may be asking for explanations (the disciples have been asking questions throughout: 13:36; 14:5, 8, 22; 16:17-18) rather than petitionary prayer. After the resurrection, the disciples will understand — no more questions about where Jesus is going or what the "little while" means. A new mode of prayer (petitionary, in Jesus's name) replaces the questioning mode of the Farewell Discourse.

"I came from the Father and entered the world; now I am leaving the world and going back to the Father" — the most compressed summary of the Incarnation and Ascension in John's Gospel. The symmetry is perfect: from the Father → into the world → leaving the world → to the Father. The disciples' response (v.29-30: "now you are speaking clearly") is ironic — they think they finally understand, but they are about to scatter (v.32).

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Augustine: "In this world you will have trouble. He did not say 'you may have' or 'you might have' — he said 'you will have.' This is certainty, not possibility. But take heart: I have overcome the world. Your troubles are certain; my victory is also certain. And you are in me. Therefore you are in the victory."

Chrysostom: "A woman giving birth forgets the anguish. Not 'she remembers it but does not mind' — she forgets it. The joy is so complete that the pain is as if it had never been. So with the resurrection: the grief of the cross will not merely be outweighed by Easter joy — it will be swallowed up in it."

hist.context:
The Childbirth Image
The woman in labour (v.21) is one of the most accessible images in the Farewell Discourse. The pain is real but temporary; the joy that follows is proportionate to the pain and erases its memory. Jesus applies this to the disciples' imminent grief at the cross: the crucifixion will seem like pure loss; the resurrection will transform it into the occasion of irreversible joy. The image also echoes OT birth-pain imagery for eschatological transformation (Isa 26:17-18; 66:7-9; Mic 4:9-10).

"I Have Overcome the World"
The final sentence of the Farewell Discourse is its summary. Everything Jesus has said — about the Spirit's coming, about the world's hatred, about prayer in his name, about peace — rests on this foundation: the world has been overcome. The perfect tense ("I have overcome") means the victory is present and permanent, not merely future. The disciples can have peace "in me" because "in me" is the location of the overcoming.

ppl.name: Jesus
ppl.text: the Spirit proves sin is fundamentally the refusal to believe in Jesus (the root of all sin)…

ppl.name: disciples
ppl.text: Jesus applies this to the disciples' imminent grief at the cross: the crucifixion will seem like pure loss…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationelenchō (16:8)NIV: "prove wrong"; KJV: "reprove"; ESV: "convict"; NRSV: "prove wrong". NIV/NRSV's "prove wrong" captures the forensic exposure of false verdicts; KJV/ESV's "reprove/convict" emphasises the personal dimension. The forensic sense is primary.nenikikā (16:33)NIV: "I have overcome"; KJV: "I have overcome"; ESV: "I have overcome"; NRSV: "I have conquered". Universal agreement on the perfect tense. NRSV's "conquered" preserves the military resonance of nikaō.

src.title: Isaiah 66:7-9
src.quote: "Before she goes into labour, she gives birth; before the pains come upon her, she delivers a son... I will not cause pain without allowing something new to be born, says the Lord."
src.note: The OT birth-pain/new-birth pattern that Jesus invokes in 16:21.

src.title: 1 John 5:4-5
src.quote: "For everyone born of God overcomes the world. This is the victory that has overcome the world, even our faith. Who is it that overcomes the world? Only the one who believes that Jesus is the Son of God."
src.note: John's epistolary extension of the "overcoming" theme from John 16:33 to the community of believers.

rec.who: "In This World You Will Have Trouble" — Christian Realism
rec.text: John 16:33 is one of the NT's clearest rejections of prosperity theology and pain-free Christianity. Jesus does not promise the absence of thlipsis; he promises the presence of the overcomer. Dietrich Bonhoeffer's Cost of Discipleship and C.S. Lewis's The Problem of Pain both draw on this verse's structure: the honest acknowledgment of suffering within the framework of a completed victory. The verse has been inscribed on hospital chapel walls, prison cell walls, and military chaplains' stoles worldwide.

rec.who: "I Have Overcome the World" in Christian Worship
rec.text: Revelation's "overcoming" language (Rev 2-3: "to him who overcomes...") develops directly from John 16:33, applying Christ's victory to the believer's participation in it. The Christus Victor theme — Christ as the warrior who has defeated the world, sin, and death — is the oldest atonement motif in Christian theology and has its Johannine root here. The triumph that looks like defeat at the cross is the victory that the empty tomb reveals.

rows.label: Grief/Joy Contrast — vv.20-24
rows.text: The childbirth image structures the passage: labour pain (cross grief) → delivery (resurrection) → permanent joy. The three movements mirror the Gospel's own structure: the long preparation of the ministry → the apparent defeat of the passion → the irreversible joy of Easter. The personal ("no one will take away your joy") and the corporate dimensions of this joy are both in view.

rows.label: Departure Reversal — vv.7, 16-22
rows.text: John structures chapter 16 around a paradox: departure (the source of grief) is simultaneously the condition for the Paraclete's arrival (the source of greater ministry). The "little while" language (vv.16-19) creates deliberate temporal ambiguity — referring simultaneously to the crucifixion, resurrection, and ultimate return.

hebtext.note: The Spirit's threefold work (v.8) uses elenchō — a legal term meaning to expose the truth of a matter, to convict through cross-examination. The Spirit does not merely inform or teach; he cross-examines the world's assumptions about sin, righteousness, and judgment, exposing each as fundamentally wrong. The world thinks sin is anything but unbelief in Jesus; the Spirit proves it is precisely that. The world thinks righteousness belongs to those who crucified Jesus; the Spirit proves it belongs to the one who rose and went to the Father. The world thinks Jesus stands condemned; the Spirit proves the prince of this world is condemned.

The Spirit "will guide you into all the truth" (v.13) — hodēgeō (to lead the way, to guide on a path). The image is of a guide leading through unfamiliar territory. The Spirit does not hand the disciples a map; he walks with them into truth. The promise is progressive and relational: the Spirit guides into all truth over time, not all at once.

The perfect tense (nenikikā — I have conquered, with present continuing effect) is one of the most theologically weighted tenses in John. Jesus speaks from the threshold of the passion as if the victory is already complete — because in the divine perspective it is. The present grief of the disciples and the coming cross are both already encompassed by a victory that has been won. The word nikē (victory) appears only here in John's Gospel but will dominate 1 John (2:13-14; 4:4; 5:4-5) and Revelation.

The joy theme (chairō, chara) runs through vv.20-24 — seven uses in five verses. The grief/joy contrast is set up by the childbirth image (v.21): the pain of labour gives way to the joy of birth. The disciples' grief at the cross will give way to the joy of the resurrection. The joy will be permanent ("no one will take away your joy," v.22) — unlike the world's joy, which is circumstantial.

thread.text: Pentecost — the arrival of the Advocate Jesus promised, fulfilling 16:7.
"When the people heard this, they were cut to the heart" — the Spirit's conviction of sin through Peter's Pentecost sermon, fulfilling 16:8-9.
"For those who are led by the Spirit of God are the children of God" — Paul's application of the Spirit's guiding work (16:13).
"As a pregnant woman about to give birth writhes and cries out in her pain, so were we in your presence, Lord" — the OT birth-pain/new-birth imagery.

tx.title: "Because I am going to the Father"
tx.content: Some MSS add "because I am going to the Father" at the end of v.16 (harmonising with v.17). NA28 omits the phrase from v.16 itself as likely borrowed from v.17 by scribes. The shorter reading is original.
tx.note: The omission is almost certain; the phrase was added by scribes to complete the parallel.

debate.title: What does "I have overcome the world" mean?

positions.name: Completed victory: the cross as the decisive defeat of evil
positions.proponents: Aulen (Christus Victor), most evangelical and orthodox scholars
positions.argument: The perfect tense declares a completed victory. The cross and resurrection constitute the decisive defeat of the world-system, Satan, sin, and death. The victory is ontologically complete; its full manifestation awaits the parousia.

positions.name: Moral victory: Jesus's faithfulness under pressure
positions.argument: "Overcoming the world" means maintaining integrity and love despite the world's opposition — a moral example rather than a cosmic event. This reading underweights both the perfect tense and the cosmic scope of "world" (kosmos) in John.

positions.name: Eschatological victory: not yet completed
positions.argument: The victory is proleptic — declared in anticipation of the final eschatological completion. It is certain but not yet fully realised.

themes.note: John 16 completes the Farewell Discourse with its most forward-looking material: the Spirit's public work of conviction (vv.8-11), the sorrow-to-joy transition of the resurrection (vv.20-24), and the victorious declaration that closes everything (v.33). The chapter moves from the disciples' confusion (v.17-18) through Jesus's patient explanation to the climactic summary: in this world, tribulation; in me, the peace of the one who has already overcome. The Farewell Discourse ends not with comfort alone but with the theological ground of comfort: a completed conquest.

coaching.tip: 'It is for your good that I am going away' (v. 7). The disciples can't imagine anything better than Jesus' presence. Yet his departure sends the Spirit—better, because universally present, internally active.

'In this world you will have trouble. But take heart! I have overcome the world' (v. 33). Trouble guaranteed; victory secured. Jesus speaks 'peace' while heading to crucifixion. His confidence transcends circumstances.
